title: Misan Harriman: Protests are full of hope
gist: Photographer and Southbank chair Misan Harriman argues protest movements are grassroots expressions of hope misrepresented as 'hate marches', warns of eroding UK protest rights under Labour, and describes surviving a coordinated press campaign against him.

insights.claim: Harriman argues his protest photographs contradict the press framing of Gaza demonstrations as 'hate marches', depicting grandmothers with Zimmer frames, Irish, Muslim and a large Jewish bloc.
insights.speaker: Imaan Irfan
insights.so_what: A challenge to dominant media narratives about pro-Palestinian protest composition.
insights.locator: These protests have sometimes been framed

insights.claim: Harriman believes exposure to people with different experiences de-radicalises, claiming to know former neo-Nazi white supremacists who now march for Gaza, climate action and women's rights.
insights.speaker: Imaan Irfan
insights.so_what: An optimistic contact-hypothesis view of political de-polarisation.
insights.locator: Most people are not evil and wicked

insights.claim: Rather than repeal the Conservative government's sweeping anti-protest laws, the Labour administration is attempting to expand them via the Crime and Policing Bill 2025, granting police more powers to restrict protest.
insights.speaker: Imaan Irfan
insights.so_what: Signals continuity of restrictive protest law under Labour—a civil-liberties trajectory to watch.
insights.locator: He is concerned about the erosion

insights.claim: Over one three-week period the Telegraph and Times alone published 14 pieces against Harriman, and some MPs called for his sacking as chair of the Southbank Centre after a decontextualised clip circulated.
insights.speaker: Imaan Irfan
insights.so_what: Illustrates the intensity of press-driven pressure campaigns against public cultural figures.

insights.claim: More than 250 cultural figures signed a letter supporting Harriman, and he will step down as Southbank Centre chair at the end of his current term in the autumn.
insights.speaker: Imaan Irfan
insights.so_what: Marks a contested leadership exit at a major UK cultural institution.

insights.claim: Harriman is a self-taught photographer who only started at 40, rose to prominence with George Floyd-era protest portraits, and became the first black photographer to shoot the cover of British Vogue.
insights.speaker: Imaan Irfan
insights.so_what: Context on a rapidly ascendant figure bridging celebrity portraiture and protest reportage.
